comment: ELEMENTS DATA FILE - PURE ELEMENT IDENTITIES
description: This file stores only core element data: identity, personality, and visual properties. Relationships and stories are stored in separate files.
usage: This is the primary source of truth for element profiles. Each element has an ID, name, symbol, atomic number, personality traits, catchphrase, fun facts, origin story, and daily life description.

fields.name: Full element name (e.g., 'Oxygen')
fields.color: Theme color hex code for UI styling
fields.category: Element category: metal, nonmetal, metalloid, noble-gas, alkali-metal, etc.
fields.catchphrase: Memorable quote said by the element
fields.dailyLife: What the element does in Element City

hydrogen.name: Hydrogen
hydrogen.catchphrase: I'm the lightest and the brightest!
hydrogen.funFact: Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe—it makes up about 90% of all atoms!
hydrogen.originStory: Hydrogen was first recognized as a distinct element by Henry Cavendish in 1766. But scientists had been making hydrogen gas for years before that—Robert Boyle produced it way back in 1671 by mixing iron and acids! The name comes from the Greek words 'hydro' (water) and 'genes' (forming), because when hydrogen burns, it forms water.
hydrogen.dailyLife: I'm an explorer and adventurer! I float around Element City meeting everyone I can. I'm so light that I can drift up to the highest towers and see everything. I love making new friends and going on exciting journeys!

helium.name: Helium
helium.funFact: Helium was discovered on the Sun before it was found on Earth!
helium.originStory: In 1868, a French astronomer named Pierre Janssen saw a mysterious yellow line in the Sun's spectrum during a solar eclipse. An English astronomer, Norman Lockyer, realized this line didn't match any known element and named it helium after Helios, the Greek god of the Sun! It wasn't until 1895 that Scottish chemist William Ramsay finally found helium on Earth by treating a mineral called clevite with acids.
helium.dailyLife: I'm the city's balloon inflator and party helper! I'm very shy, but I love making children smile by making things float. I'm the second most abundant element in the universe, but I keep a low profile.

carbon.name: Carbon
carbon.catchphrase: I can be anything you want me to be!
carbon.funFact: All life on Earth is based on carbon—you're made of it, plants are made of it, even stars use it for energy!
carbon.originStory: Carbon has been known to humans since prehistoric times! Ancient people used charcoal, soot, and coal without knowing they were the same element. In the 17th century, Robert Boyle recognized carbon as an element. Later, Antoine Lavoisier discovered that diamonds are made of carbon, and Carl Wilhelm Scheele proved that graphite is also a form of carbon. The name comes from the Latin word 'carbo,' meaning charcoal.
carbon.dailyLife: I'm the city's artist and shapeshifter! I can be a beautiful, hard diamond or soft, dark graphite. I can even be buckyballs (C₆₀) that look like soccer balls! I love creating new forms and surprising everyone with my versatility.

nitrogen.name: Nitrogen
nitrogen.catchphrase: I'm everywhere, but no one notices me!
nitrogen.funFact: About 78% of the air you breathe is nitrogen!
nitrogen.originStory: Nitrogen was discovered in 1772 by Daniel Rutherford, a Scottish physician. He removed oxygen and carbon dioxide from air and showed that the leftover gas wouldn't support burning or life—he called it 'noxious air.' Other scientists like Scheele, Cavendish, and Priestley also studied it around the same time. The name comes from 'nitre' (a mineral) and 'genes' (forming), because it's found in nitrates.
nitrogen.dailyLife: I'm the quiet backbone of Element City. Most people don't notice me, but I make up most of the air! I help plants grow through the nitrogen cycle and keep things stable. I'm calm, reliable, and always there when you need me.

oxygen.name: Oxygen
oxygen.funFact: Oxygen makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere and is the most abundant element in Earth's crust.
oxygen.originStory: Oxygen was discovered around 1772 by a Swedish chemist named Carl Wilhelm Scheele, who made it by heating various substances. Then in 1774, an English chemist named Joseph Priestley independently discovered it by heating mercuric oxide with sunlight focused through a magnifying glass! A French scientist named Antoine Lavoisier gave it the name 'oxygen' from Greek words meaning 'acid former,' because he thought all acids contained oxygen.
oxygen.dailyLife: I'm the city's firefighter and life-support specialist! I help living things breathe and safely assist with combustion. I'm friends with almost everyone—I react with practically every other element! I love being helpful, but sometimes I cause a little trouble (looking at you, Iron and your rust!).

sodium.name: Sodium
sodium.funFact: Sodium is so reactive that it can ignite on contact with water!
sodium.originStory: Although sodium compounds like salt (sodium chloride) have been used for thousands of years, pure sodium wasn't isolated until 1807. Sir Humphry Davy, an English chemist, made it by running electricity through molten sodium hydroxide (caustic soda). The name comes from the English word 'soda,' and the symbol Na comes from the Latin word 'natrium.'
sodium.dailyLife: I'm the city's streetlamp lighter! I produce a brilliant yellow light when excited—you've seen me in streetlights! I'm dramatic and love attention, but I have to be careful around water. I make table salt with my best friend Chlorine!

chlorine.name: Chlorine
chlorine.funFact: Chlorine is a greenish-yellow gas and was used to make drinking water safe for millions of people.
chlorine.originStory: Chlorine was first produced in 1774 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele (the same chemist who discovered oxygen) by mixing the mineral pyrolusite with hydrochloric acid. Scheele thought the greenish gas contained oxygen, but in 1810, Sir Humphry Davy proved it was actually a distinct element and named it chlorine from the Greek word 'chloros,' meaning greenish-yellow.
chlorine.dailyLife: I'm the city's water treatment specialist and pool cleaner! I keep drinking water safe and swimming pools clean. I combine directly with nearly every element, so I'm never found free in nature. I'm powerful and protective, but I can be dangerous if not handled carefully!

iron.name: Iron
iron.funFact: The oldest known iron objects were made from meteorites that fell from space!
iron.originStory: Iron has been known to humans since prehistoric times—it's one of the oldest metals we've ever used. The earliest iron tools date back to around 4000 BC, and people first got iron from meteorites that landed on Earth. The ancient Egyptians and Sumerians tipped their spears with meteorite iron! By 3000 BC, people had learned to smelt iron from ore.
iron.dailyLife: I'm the city's builder and construction worker! I'm strong, reliable, and I make buildings, bridges, and tools. I'm the backbone of civilization! I work hard every day and I'm proud of what I build. Just don't remind me about that time I got rusty... it's a sensitive topic.

gold.name: Gold
gold.catchphrase: I've been treasured for thousands of years!
gold.funFact: All the gold ever mined would fit in a cube just 22 meters wide! And it's edible—it has no taste and isn't toxic!
gold.originStory: Gold is one of the very first metals ever discovered by humans. It's been valued since the earliest recorded times—the Egyptians were smelting gold over 5,600 years ago! The name comes from the Anglo-Saxon word 'gold,' and the symbol Au comes from the Latin word 'aurum.' It's found in nature in pure form, so early peoples could just pick it up and use it.
gold.dailyLife: I'm the city's historian and banker. I've been around for thousands of years and I remember everything. I'm wise, ancient, and have seen civilizations rise and fall. I'm valuable and I know it, but I've earned the right to be proud. I even help astronauts—my layer on their helmets reflects the Sun's rays!

mercury.name: Mercury
mercury.funFact: Mercury is the only common metal that's liquid at room temperature—it's also called quicksilver!
mercury.originStory: Mercury has been known to humans since ancient times. It was found in Egyptian tombs dating from 1500 BC, and the ancient Chinese and Hindus knew about it too. The name comes from the Roman god Mercury, known for his speed and mobility—perfect for a liquid metal! A mercury mine in Spain called Almadén has been worked for over 2,500 years.
mercury.dailyLife: I'm the city's mysterious liquid metal! I slither and slide everywhere, always changing shape. I'm ancient and full of secrets. I used to be in thermometers, but now I'm more of a retired mystery—I let my friend alcohol take over that job. I'm the only common metal that's liquid at room temperature, and I'm proud of it!
